EEE 465/591: Photovoltaic Energy Conversion

Mini Project #1: Solar Radiation Analysis

Student Name: Saif Elsaady
Student ID: 1222041184
Date: August 22, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Nicholas Rolston

Executive Summary

This project analyzes solar radiation data from "KANSAS CITY INT'L ARPT", MO (USAF Station 724460) using TMY3 (Typical Meteorological Year) data. The analysis includes solar position calculations, radiation on tilted surfaces, and optimization of photovoltaic module orientation. Key findings indicate that a module tilted at latitude angle (39.30°) facing south yields approximately 1645 kWh/m²/year, with peak radiation occurring in summer and minimum in winter.

1. Introduction

Solar radiation analysis is fundamental to photovoltaic system design and performance evaluation. This project develops computational tools to analyze TMY3 solar radiation data, calculate sun positions throughout the year, and determine optimal module orientations for maximum energy harvest. The analysis provides insights into seasonal variations and the impact of module tilt angles on energy production.

2. Methodology

2.1 Data Source

The analysis uses TMY3 data from the National Solar Radiation Database (NSRDB) for "KANSAS CITY INT'L ARPT", MO:

ParameterValue
Station ID724460
Location"KANSAS CITY INT'L ARPT", MO
Latitude39.30°N
Longitude94.72°W
Elevation298 m
Time ZoneUTC-6

2.2 Solar Position Calculations

Solar position is calculated using the following astronomical equations:

Declination Angle:
δ = 23.45° × sin[360°/365 × (n - 81)]

Equation of Time:
EoT = 9.87sin(2B) - 7.53cos(B) - 1.5sin(B)
where B = 360°/365 × (n - 81)

Hour Angle:
ω = 15° × (LST - 12)

Solar Elevation:
α = arcsin[sin(δ)sin(φ) + cos(δ)cos(φ)cos(ω)]

Solar Azimuth:
Az = arccos[(cos(φ)sin(δ) - cos(δ)sin(φ)cos(ω))/cos(α)]

Where: δ = declination, n = day number, φ = latitude, LST = local solar time, ω = hour angle

2.3 Module Radiation Calculations

The radiation incident on a tilted module is calculated using:

Direct Component:
I_direct = DNI × cos(θ)
where cos(θ) = sin(α)cos(β) + cos(α)sin(β)cos(Az_sun - Az_module)

Diffuse Component (Isotropic Sky Model):
I_diffuse = DHI × (180° - β)/180°

Total Radiation:
I_total = I_direct + I_diffuse

3. Results and Analysis

3.1 Task 1: TMY3 Data Visualization

Figure 1: Annual Solar Radiation Components

The three-panel plot shows hourly Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI), Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI), and Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI) for the entire year. Clear seasonal variation with peak radiation in summer months.

3.2 Task 2: Solar Position Calculations

2a. Hand Calculation Example

For June 21 (day 172) at solar noon (12:00):

Step-by-step calculation:
1. Declination: δ = 23.45°
2. Equation of Time: EoT = -1.45 minutes
3. Time Correction: TC = -20.32 minutes
4. Local Solar Time: LST = 11.66 hours
5. Hour Angle: ω = -5.08°
6. Solar Elevation: α = 73.58°
7. Solar Azimuth: Az = 163.31°

2b. Sun Path Visualization

Figure 2: Sun Path on Summer Solstice

Polar plot showing the sun's trajectory across the sky on June 21. The plot includes hour markers and uses color coding to indicate time of day. Maximum elevation occurs at solar noon.

3.3 Task 3: Tilted Module Performance

Module Configuration:
• Tilt Angle: 39.30° (equal to latitude)
• Azimuth: 180° (south-facing)

Annual Energy Production:
• Total: 1644.8 kWh/m²
• Direct Component: 1174.2 kWh/m² (71.4%)
• Diffuse Component: 470.6 kWh/m² (28.6%)

Figure 3: Hourly Radiation on Tilted Module

The plot shows radiation intensity throughout the year on the optimally tilted module. Winter months show higher relative gains compared to horizontal surfaces due to favorable sun angles.

5. Discussion

The analysis reveals several important findings for PV system design in "KANSAS CITY INT'L ARPT", MO:

  • Optimal Tilt: The latitude-tilt angle (39.30°) provides maximum annual energy production (1644.8 kWh/m²), representing a 4.9% improvement over horizontal mounting.
  • Seasonal Variation: Monthly radiation varies significantly between peak and minimum months for horizontal surfaces.
  • Direct vs. Diffuse: Direct radiation dominates, comprising 71.4% of total radiation on the tilted module, indicating good potential for concentrating PV systems.
  • Capacity Factor: The calculated capacity factor of 18.12% based on GHI is typical for this latitude and climate.

Key Performance Metrics

  • ✓ Annual Energy Yield: 1644.8 kWh/m²/year
  • ✓ Capacity Factor: 18.12%
  • ✓ Annual Diffuse Fraction: 37.9%
  • ✓ Peak Radiation Day: Day 185 with 8.41 kWh/m²
  • ✓ Minimum Radiation Day: Day 365 with 0.68 kWh/m²

6. Conclusions

This project successfully developed computational tools for analyzing solar radiation data and optimizing PV module orientation. The Python implementation provides a framework for:

  1. Processing TMY3 solar radiation data
  2. Calculating accurate sun positions throughout the year
  3. Determining radiation on arbitrarily oriented surfaces
  4. Optimizing module tilt for maximum annual energy production

The code developed is modular, well-documented, and suitable for extension in future projects. The analysis confirms that latitude-tilt mounting provides optimal year-round performance for fixed-tilt PV systems in "KANSAS CITY INT'L ARPT", MO, with potential for 1645 kWh/m²/year energy production.

References

  1. Wilcox, S. and Marion, W. (2008). "Users Manual for TMY3 Data Sets", NREL/TP-581-43156, National Renewable Energy Laboratory, Golden, CO.
  2. Duffie, J.A. and Beckman, W.A. (2013). "Solar Engineering of Thermal Processes", 4th Edition, John Wiley & Sons.
  3. Masters, G.M. (2013). "Renewable and Efficient Electric Power Systems", 2nd Edition, Wiley-IEEE Press.
  4. National Solar Radiation Database (NSRDB). Available at: https://nsrdb.nrel.gov/
  5. NREL PVWatts Calculator. Available at: https://pvwatts.nrel.gov/

Appendix A: Code Documentation

The complete Python source code includes the following key functions:

  • sun_position() - Calculates solar elevation and azimuth
  • module_direct() - Computes direct radiation on tilted surface
  • declination() - Solar declination angle calculation
  • equation_of_time() - Time correction for solar calculations

All code is commented and follows PEP 8 style guidelines for Python. The modular structure facilitates reuse in future projects. This HTML report was automatically generated by the Python script with embedded plots and complete terminal output.
